posts.text: Immigration research still matters. I'm grateful to Brian Munoz at @stlpublicradio for interviewing me on 287(g) agreements, and to everyone who has been reading and sharing my work. Here's what we covered. 🧵

I'm working with former DHS official Claire Trickler-McNulty on exactly this question "what does a detention system that doesn't rely on mass incarceration actually look like?" We discussed it here:  austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

Read/Listen to what I have to say here: 
stlpr.org/government-politic… 

stlpr.org/government-politic…

Mohammad Nazeer Paktiawal, 41, served alongside U.S. Army Special Forces in Afghanistan beginning in 2005. He was arrested by ICE on March 13, 2026, in front of his children. Less than 24 hours later, he was dead. This is his story, and why it matters. 🧵

Shawn VanDiver (@shawnjvandiver) of AfghanEvac put it plainly: "It would be pretty weird for a healthy 41-year-old man to die less than 24 hours after being taken into government custody." He is calling for an immediate, transparent, and independent investigation.

Paktiawal survived the Taliban. He did not survive ICE. That contrast demands a reckoning, not just about one man's death, but about the system that produced it. 
My full analysis and the complete dataset of detention deaths is here:
 austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

Read/Listen to what I have to say here: 
www.stlpr.org/government-p... 

www.stlpr.org/government-p...

I'm working with former DHS official Claire Trickler-McNulty on exactly this question "what does a detention system that doesn't rely on mass incarceration actually look like?" We discussed it here:  austinkocher.substack.com/p/the-altern...

The fundamental question I keep coming back to: do we actually need to detain 70,000 (or even 100,000) people? I don't think so. We cannot detain our way out of this. We need smarter, evidence-based approaches that don't rely on mass incarceration.

ICE's detained population went from under 40,000 to over 70,000 people in just 12 months, the fastest growth in the history of U.S. immigrant detention. We have never seen this many new facilities come online this quickly, and the infrastructure is struggling to keep up.

The risks are real. Evidence across many years shows the task force model can exacerbate racial profiling, lead to unlawful arrests, and generate lawsuits that cost local taxpayers significant money with oversight mechanisms now largely gutted inside DHS.

Local agencies sign on for different reasons, some see it as interagency cooperation, others as a political signal. But the Trump admin has changed the financial calculus: it now says it will pay for training time & financial incentives for enforcement activity under 287(g).

More than 60 individual 287(g) agreements have been signed in Missouri alone. In O'Fallon, a routine traffic stop landed a former DACA recipient in a jail after being handed over to ICE. These agreements make every interaction with local police a immigration enforcement event.

The task force model allows local officers to ask about immigration status during routine traffic stops and 911 calls out in the field. It is the most concerning model, and it is now the most common one being signed across the country.

There are 3 types of 287(g) agreements. The jail model lets officers check immigration status at booking. The warrant model lets officers execute immigration warrants. The task force model, now the dominant form in Missouri and nationally, is the most expansive.

Under U.S. law, local police cannot enforce federal immigration law on their own. 287(g) agreements are the contracts that change that deputizing local officers to carry out specific immigration enforcement tasks on ICE's behalf.

Immigration research still matters. I'm grateful to Brian Munoz at 
@stlpublicradio.bsky.social for interviewing me on 287(g) agreements, and to everyone who has been reading and sharing my work. Here's what we covered. 🧵

Paktiawal survived the Taliban. He did not survive ICE. That contrast demands a reckoning, not just about one man's death, but about the system that produced it. 
My full analysis and the complete dataset of detention deaths is here:
 austinkocher.substack.com/p/ice-detent...

Shawn VanDiver of AfghanEvac put it plainly: "It would be pretty weird for a healthy 41-year-old man to die less than 24 hours after being taken into government custody." He is calling for an immediate, transparent, and independent investigation.

Death in ICE detention is no longer a shocking rarity. It has become a predictable, normalized feature of agency operations, while Congress and much of the American public remains unaware or indifferent to what is happening inside these facilities.

Since the start of the second Trump administration, 41 people have died in ICE detention. That count comes from ICE's own press releases. It does not include people killed by agents outside of detention, or those who died fleeing enforcement.

Paktiawal is the 12th person to die in ICE custody in 2026. Since January 1, ICE detention has taken a life once every six days on average. At this rate, we are on pace for more than 60 deaths in ICE custody by the end of this year alone.

ICE's statement described him as a "criminal illegal alien" and cited two arrests, for SNAP fraud and theft, neither of which resulted in a conviction. According to AfghanEvac, those charges had never been heard by a court at the time of his death.

Paktiawal had completed his asylum interview with U.S. immigration authorities and held a valid work authorization and Social Security number. His asylum case was still pending at the time of his arrest. He had not been convicted of any crime.

That evening, Paktiawal complained of shortness of breath and chest pains. ICE transported him to Parkland Hospital, where an ER doctor recommended he stay for observation. Early on March 14, staff noticed his tongue had swollen. He was declared dead at 9:10 a.m.

On the morning of March 13, 8 masked federal agents surrounded Paktiawal in his vehicle outside his home in Richardson, Texas, as he was preparing to take his children to school. He was taken to the ICE Dallas Field Office processing hold room.
